items.q: What is the difference between a strong acid and a concentrated acid?
items.correct: A strong acid fully dissociates in water; a concentrated acid has a high amount of acid per volume — these are independent properties.
items.misconception: Strong and concentrated mean the same thing for acids.
items.random_wrong: A strong acid is one that has been heated to higher temperature.
items.why: In everyday language 'strong' and 'concentrated' are synonyms (e.g. strong coffee), so students transfer that meaning to chemistry.

items.q: When an acid neutralises an alkali, what is the pH of the resulting solution?
items.correct: Approximately 7 if equivalent amounts of strong acid and strong alkali react — but it varies with strength.
items.misconception: Neutralisation always produces a solution with pH exactly 7 regardless of the acid and base used.
items.random_wrong: Neutralisation always produces a solution with pH 0.
items.why: Students learn the rule 'neutral = 7' so rigidly that they ignore weak-acid/weak-base cases where the salt is acidic or basic.

items.q: What is the relationship between two enantiomers?
items.correct: They are non-superimposable mirror images that rotate plane-polarised light in opposite directions but have identical chemical and physical properties otherwise.
items.misconception: Enantiomers have different boiling points and react completely differently with all reagents.
items.random_wrong: Enantiomers are the same molecule drawn differently on paper.
items.why: Students don't realise enantiomers are chemically indistinguishable except in chiral environments (light, biological receptors); they assume all isomers must behave differently.

items.q: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 7. “10” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Hydrochloric acid (HCl). “Ethanoic acid” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Universal indicator in a solution turns dark purple. Which pH value is most likely?
items.why: The real figure is 13. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
